attributes.stem:
A 40-year-old gardener presents to his GP with a solitary painless lesion on his finger. Upon further questioning, the patient describes that the "lump" is only now bothering him as it is preventing him from easily holding his tools while gardening. It has been present for about 5 weeks. The patient does not recall any specific trigger but does mention regularly sustaining minor cuts or bruises from his work. He reports no other medical history, no history of similar lesions in his life, and receives regular skin checks. The patient has never smoked cigarettes.

Upon physical examination, the patient is well with observations within normal limits. The lesion itself is non-tender and is located on the volar surface of the left 2nd digit. (Image)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The correct answer is pyogenic granuloma. A pyogenic granuloma is a form of benign vascular tumour. Due to its vascular nature, it tends to be complicated by recurring bleeding. It is often seen in children, pregnant women but can also occur following minor trauma. It tends to favour growth over hands, feet and mucosal surfaces of mouth and perianal skin. The name 'pyogenic granuloma' is misleading as the lesion is not due to infection or have granulomatous component. The mechanism of trauma is variable but commonly results from minor cuts or abrasions such as with rose thorn pricks. One characteristic that is common of pyogenic granulomas is the rate of growth over weeks to months.

Basal cell carcinomas (BCC) are slow-growing plaques or nodules with a characteristically smooth surface, rolled border, and telangiectasia. BCCs are commonly associated with sun-damage and UV exposure. Therefore it is unlikely this patient's first lesion would be on the volar aspect of his hand which is typically shielded from the sun.

Keratoacanthomas are typically identified by the presence of a keratin-filled core. The image of the lesion does not resemble a typical keratoacanthoma.

Warts can present at any site on the body and hence is a plausible answer. Most warts on acral skin tends to have scaly thickened overlying surface, unlikely mucosal warts which can be fleshy in nature.

Amelanotic melanomas may present similarly to this patient but is much less common than pyogenic granuloma. The lesion in the photograph does not represent a typical amelanotic melanoma and the patient does not possess typical risk factors including old age, smoking history, and a lesion in an area of significant skin exposure. In practice, the lesion would likely be biopsied and analysed for any malignant findings.
learningPoints: Pyogenic granuloma are rapidly growing benign lesions of vascular origin.
references: Plachouri KM, Georgiou S.. Therapeutic approaches to pyogenic granuloma: an updated review. Int J Dermatol. 2019; 58:642-48.
